1: 'Then the LORD said to me: Take a large writing tablet and write on it in common script: Maher-shalal-hash-baz' — the third prophetic sign after the Immanuel sign (Isaiah 7:14) and Shear-jashub (Isaiah 7:3). 'Maher-shalal-hash-baz' (meher shalal chash baz, 'speed the spoil, hasten the plunder') is the longest personal name in the OT and an announcement of imminent Assyrian conquest. The 'large writing tablet' (gillyon gadol, large tablet) inscribed in 'common script' (cheret enosh, the writing of ordinary people/common inscription) ensures public accessibility — the prophecy is written where all can read it. The public nature is the authentication: when the event happens, all can verify the prior prediction.
2: 'And I called in reliable witnesses, Uriah the priest and Zechariah son of Jeberechiah' — the legal authentication of the prophetic word: two witnesses (the minimum required by Deuteronomy 19:15 for legal establishment of a matter). Uriah the priest appears in 2 Kings 16:10-16 as the priest who built an Assyrian altar for Ahaz — a collaborator with the king's pro-Assyrian policy. Zechariah son of Jeberechiah is otherwise unknown. The witnesses are from the establishment — not Isaiah's disciples but public figures who will be around to testify when the prophecy is fulfilled.
3: 'Then I went to the prophetess, and she conceived and bore a son. The LORD said to me: Name him Maher-shalal-hash-baz' — 'the prophetess' (haneviah, the female prophet) is almost certainly Isaiah's wife rather than a title for an unrelated female prophet. The birth is not miraculous (unlike the Immanuel sign of Isaiah 7:14) but a normal conception and birth. The child named with the prophetic name is the third sign-child: Shear-jashub (Isaiah 7:3: remnant will return), Immanuel (Isaiah 7:14: God with us), and Maher-shalal-hash-baz (speed the plunder). Isaiah's family becomes a walking prophetic message.
4: 'For before the child knows how to say My father or My mother, the wealth of Damascus and the plunder of Samaria will be carried off before the king of Assyria' — the timing sign: before the infant can speak 'abi' (my father) or 'immi' (my mother) — the first words of a nursing child. This is approximately one year of age. Within that timeframe, Damascus's wealth and Samaria's spoil will be carried to Assyria. Historical fulfillment: Tiglath-Pileser III captured Damascus and deported its population in 732 BCE, and the northern kingdom's capital Samaria fell in 722 BCE. The name 'speed the plunder, hasten the spoil' is the event compressed into a child's name.
5: 'The LORD spoke to me again, saying' — the brief narrative break (Isaiah 8:5) before the 'waters of Shiloah' oracle marks the transition from the sign-child narrative (Isaiah 8:1-4) to the interpretive oracle (Isaiah 8:6-8). The 'again' suggests continued divine communication — Isaiah is the ongoing recipient of prophetic word during this period of Assyrian threat. The oracle that follows (Isaiah 8:6-8) interprets the present political situation in terms of water: Israel chose the wrong water source.
6: 'Because this people has rejected the waters of Shiloah that flow gently, and rejoice over Rezin and the son of Remaliah' — the waters of Shiloah (mei hashiloach, the waters of Siloam) flowed gently (haholekhim le'at, walking slowly) through Jerusalem as a gentle, reliable supply. In John 9:7, 11, the Pool of Siloam is where Jesus sends the blind man to wash — 'Siloam' meaning 'sent.' The gentle waters of Shiloah = YHWH's quiet, covenant provision for Jerusalem. This people rejected the gentle water and 'rejoiced over Rezin and Remaliah's son' — the coalition that threatened Jerusalem. The rejection of divine provision for confidence in human political alliances is the core accusation.
7: 'Therefore, behold, the Lord is bringing up against them the mighty floodwaters of the River — the king of Assyria and all his power. It will rise over all its channels and overflow all its banks' — the contrasting waters: the gentle Shiloah vs. the Euphrates (hanahur haatsum veharav, the mighty and great river). The Euphrates in full flood overflows its banks (all its channels — kulnahalav, all its wadis). The geographic logic: rejecting the gentle local water in favor of dependence on the great river power (Assyria = the Euphrates River civilization) will result in being flooded by that great river. The river-flood metaphor (Assyrian armies as rising waters) is developed in Jeremiah 46:7-8 and Habakkuk 3:8.
8: 'Sweeping through Judah, overflowing as it goes, reaching even to the neck; and with its outspread wings it will fill the entire breadth of your land, O Immanuel' — the flood metaphor extends: the Assyrian flood sweeps through Judah reaching 'to the neck' (ad-tsavar) — nearly submerging but not quite. The neck-level flood means Jerusalem is barely above water: surrounded, threatened, almost overwhelmed but not destroyed. Sennacherib's 701 BCE campaign literally did this — 46 cities destroyed, Hezekiah 'like a bird in a cage' (Taylor Prism) but Jerusalem not taken. 'O Immanuel' — the land addressed by the name of Isaiah 7:14: God is with us in the flood. The Immanuel name is the guarantee of non-destruction even in the midst of near-submersion.
9: 'Band yourselves together, O peoples, and you will be broken in pieces; give ear, all you distant lands: arm yourselves and you will be broken in pieces; arm yourselves and you will be broken in pieces!' — the defiant challenge to the nations: three-fold 'you will be broken to pieces' (ro'u, be shattered). The rhetorical triple intensifies: 'band together' (ro'u, be hostile), 'arm yourselves' (hitallu, arm/gird yourselves), 'arm yourselves again' — each attempt at hostile mobilization is met with the same counter: shattering. The confidence here is based on Immanuel (Isaiah 8:8 and Isaiah 8:10: 'God is with us'): the nations' plans are futile when the God of Zion is present.
10: 'Devise your plans — they will come to nothing; speak your word — it will not stand, for God is with us' — 'God is with us' (ki immanu El) unpacks the name Immanuel (immanu El, God-with-us, Isaiah 7:14). The nations' plans (otsu etsah, take counsel) and their word (dabru davar, speak the word) will both fail: it will not stand (velo yaqum, it will not arise/stand). The same formula as Isaiah 7:7 (lo taqum velo tihyeh, it will not stand and will not be). The theological ground for political confidence: Immanuel — the divine presence with his people — makes human coalition-building ultimately futile against Jerusalem.
11: 'For the LORD spoke to me with his strong hand upon me, and warned me not to walk in the way of this people, saying' — 'with his strong hand upon me' (be'chezqat hayad, with the strength of the hand) is the language of prophetic compulsion: the divine hand that came upon Ezekiel (Ezekiel 1:3; Isaiah 3:14, 22; Isaiah 8:1; Isaiah 37:1; Isaiah 40:1) and Elijah (1 Kings 18:46). Isaiah is being warned against a specific path: the way of 'this people' (ha'am hazeh, the same dismissive phrase as Isaiah 6:9-10). The prophet is being called out of the crowd — marked as distinct from the prevailing political and theological consensus.
12: 'Do not call a conspiracy everything this people calls a conspiracy; do not fear what they fear, and do not dread it' — the prophetic counter-culture: Isaiah is not to adopt the fear-vocabulary of the populace. 'Conspiracy' (qesher, a plot/conspiracy) — the people see political conspiracy everywhere; Isaiah is not to adopt their conspiratorial framework. The specific fears may be the Syro-Ephraimite threat or the anticipated Assyrian response to Judah's position. The prophetic calling involves a different epistemology: seeing events through YHWH's perspective rather than through the anxiety of political calculation.
13: 'But the LORD of hosts — him you shall regard as holy; let him be your fear, and let him be your dread' — the substitution of fears: YHWH replaces the political/military fears of the populace. 'Regard as holy' (taqidishu, you will sanctify/set apart) — acknowledging YHWH's holiness (as in the trisagion of Isaiah 6:3) is the cognitive act that relocates fear. 'Your fear' (morakchem, your reverence/fear) and 'your dread' (umor'akchem, what you dread) — the emotional responses appropriate to danger are redirected from the Assyrian army to YHWH. Proverbs 1:7 and the wisdom tradition: the fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom; all other fears are derivative and manageable.
14: 'He will be a sanctuary, but also a stone that people stumble over, a rock that makes them fall — a trap and a snare for both houses of Israel and for the inhabitants of Jerusalem' — the two effects of YHWH's holiness: sanctuary (miqdash, holy place/refuge) for those who fear him, stumbling-stone (even negef, stone of stumbling) and rock of offense (tsur michshol, rock that causes falling) for both houses of Israel. The stone metaphor applied to YHWH himself: Paul in Romans 9:33 combines this verse with Psalms 118:22 (the cornerstone) to explain Israel's rejection of the gospel: 'a stone that causes people to stumble and a rock that makes them fall.' 1 Peter 2:8 applies the same combination to Christ. The stone of YHWH/Christ is simultaneously foundation and stumbling-block.
15: 'And many among them will stumble; they will fall and be broken; they will be snared and captured' — the three verbs of stumbling/disaster for those who reject YHWH's lordship: kashlu (they will stumble), venapelu venishbaru (they will fall and be broken/shattered), venoqushu venilkadu (they will be snared and captured). The progression from stumbling to capture is the escalating catastrophe that follows YHWH being a stumbling-stone rather than a sanctuary. The snare/trap imagery (moqesh vepach) is the hunter's language — YHWH as the trapper of those who reject him is also in Psalms 91:3 (YHWH delivering from the fowler's trap) applied inversely.
16: 'Bind up the testimony; seal the teaching among my disciples' — the formation of Isaiah's prophetic circle: the testimony (te'udah, the witnessed/attested word) is bound (tsror, tie/wrap up) and the teaching/torah (torah, instruction) is sealed (chatom, sealed with a seal) among Isaiah's disciples (limmudai, my learned ones). The sealing is for preservation until the prophecy is fulfilled — a future generation will be the audience for the message rejected in the present. This verse may explain the editorial history of Isaiah: a school of disciples preserved and expanded the master's prophecies. The same concept appears in Isaiah 30:8 ('write it on a tablet and inscribe it in a book so that it may be for the time to come as a witness forever').
17: 'I will wait for the LORD, who is hiding his face from the house of Jacob, and I will hope in him' — the hiding of the divine face (hamesater panav, the one who hides his face) during the Assyrian period is the prophetic equivalent of the lament psalms (Psalms 13:1: 'How long will you hide your face from me?'; Isaiah 27:9: 'Do not hide your face from me'). Isaiah's personal posture of waiting and hoping (veyichalti lo, I will hope/wait for him) while YHWH hides his face is the prophetic spirituality of the interval between word and fulfillment. The waiting prophet becomes the model for the believing community that waits for YHWH's hidden face to turn again.
18: 'Here am I, and the children the LORD has given me. We are signs and wonders in Israel from the LORD of hosts who dwells on Mount Zion' — Isaiah and his sign-children (Shear-jashub, Maher-shalal-hash-baz, and implicitly Immanuel) constitute a living symbolic system: signs (otot) and wonders (moftim) from YHWH. Hebrews 2:13 cites this verse (along with Psalms 22:22) to show Jesus as the one who sanctifies his brothers, sharing in their humanity: 'Here am I, and the children God has given me.' The application: Jesus and his people are the new prophetic family — signs and wonders from YHWH. 'Who dwells on Mount Zion' grounds the prophetic authority in YHWH's specific location: the Jerusalem temple.
19: 'When people say to you: Consult the mediums and the spiritists who whisper and mutter — should not a people consult their God? Why should the living seek guidance from the dead?' — the anti-necromancy polemic: in the crisis of the Assyrian threat, consulting mediums (ha'ovot, the spirits of the dead conjured by mediums — from 'ov, a ghost) and spiritists (hayide'onim, those who know/summon the dead) is offered as a substitute for YHWH's word. Necromancy was common in the ANE (Mesopotamian and Egyptian necromantic practices are well attested) and explicitly prohibited in Deuteronomy 18:10-11 and Leviticus 19:31; Isaiah 20:6. 'Why should the living seek guidance from the dead?' (lamedaber el-hameth, to speak to the dead) — the rhetorical logic is stark: dead spirits are not sources of living guidance.
20: 'To the teaching and to the testimony! If anyone does not speak according to this word, it is because there is no light in them' — the canonical criterion: Torah and testimony (letorah velite'udah, to the instruction and to the witnessed word) are the standard by which all other guidance is tested. The mediums and spiritists of Isaiah 8:19 fail this test. 'No light in them' (sheyn lo shachar, there is no dawn in them) — 'shachar' (dawn/morning light) is not just physical light but enlightenment, understanding, divine communication. The medium who does not align with Torah has no divine light — their words come from a different source entirely.
21: 'They will roam through the land, hard-pressed and famished; and when hunger seizes them, they will be enraged and will curse their king and their God, and look upward' — the consequence for those who reject Torah and pursue mediums: wandering in hunger and want. 'Curse their king and their God' (veqillel bemalko uvelohav) — in the extremity of suffering, the unfaithful curse YHWH and the king rather than repenting. The looking upward (venabbet la'maalah) from Isaiah 8:21 is paired with the looking earthward of Isaiah 8:22 — neither direction offers relief, because the rejection of YHWH's word has closed off both the divine source above and the earthly world below.
22: 'And then look to the earth and see nothing but distress and darkness, the gloom of anguish, and they will be thrust into thick darkness' — the closing darkness image echoes 5:30 and prepares for the great light of Isaiah 9:2 ('the people walking in darkness have seen a great light'). 'Distress and darkness' (tsarah vechoshekh), 'gloom of anguish' (me'uf mutsaq), and 'thick darkness' (afelah) — four synonyms for darkness/despair pile up. The darkness is both literal (the crisis of Assyrian invasion) and spiritual (the consequence of rejecting YHWH's light). The next verse (Isaiah 9:1) begins: 'But there will be no more gloom for those who were in distress' — the contrast pivot from darkness (Isaiah 7-8) to light (Isaiah 9:1-7) is one of Isaiah's greatest literary and theological movements.
